text: This is a video of a thirty four-year-old female undergoing removal of a fallopian tube prolapse.
She had undergone vaginal hysterectomy elsewhere and had three unsuccessful vaginal attempts at removal of a fallopian tube prolapse.
Her initial presenting complaints or a watery discharge and pain with intercourse.
After the second removal, she developed a CT-evidenced abscess in the right fallopian tube and ovary.
This was treated conservatively with antibiotics, but failed to resolve the clear vaginal discharge.
She was referred for robotic removal of the fallopian tubal prolapse.
The surgeon observes both fallopian tubes adhered to the vaginal cuff, with the right fallopian tube identified as the problematic structure.
The surgeon observes the left fallopian tube, which appears swollen and redundant, with a sizable hydrosalpinx visible.
The surgeon employs a retroperitoneal approach lateral to the vaginal cuff, carefully avoiding the pelvic sidewall vessels and ureter.

The surgeon performs tedious blunt and sharp dissection to carefully remove the tubal remnant and hydrosalpinx from the pelvic sidewall and left ovary, separating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to fulgurate a small follicular cyst, visibly reducing its size and altering its appearance.
The surgeon leaves the relatively healthy left ovary intact while removing the fallopian tube adhered to the vaginal cuff.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter and performs a retroperitoneal approach around the infundibulopelvic vessels, ensuring careful navigation around these anatomical structures.
The surgical field shows the infundibulopelvic ligament and ovarian vessels as part of the complex being addressed.
The surgeon clamps, fulgurates, and cuts the infundibulopelvic ligament and ovarian vessels, ensuring the procedure remains well away from the identified right ureter.
The surgeon initiates a retroperitoneal approach to dissect the right ovarian and tubal abscess from the pelvic side wall, carefully separating the abscess from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visually inspects the right ureter to ensure its integrity and proper positioning during the procedure.
The surgeon continues safe lysis of adhesions around the infundibulopelvic ligament and ovarian vessels, ensuring careful separation from the identified right ureter using a retroperitoneal approach.

The surgical team encounters the assist structure while dissecting the right ovarian and tubal abscess from the pelvic side wall, ensuring careful manipulation around the infundibulopelvic ligament and ovarian vessels.
The surgeon addresses an abscess, ensuring careful dissection and separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder flap away from the vaginal cuff, separating these structures to proceed with the hysterectomy.
The fallopian tube is observed to be closely associated with both the vaginal tissue and the bladder muscularis.
Again, tedious dissection prevailed.
The surgeon performs a vaginotomy to expose and identify the tubal remnant located within the vaginal tissue.
The surgeon uses an endoanal sizer to manipulate the vagina, visually identifying the entry point for the vaginotomy.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to carefully free the fallopian tube from its attachments, facilitating its separation from the surrounding tissues.
The fallopian tube is visibly adhered to the vaginal cuff, indicating a close attachment between these structures.
The surgeon carefully teases the fallopian tubal femur away from the vaginal cuff and angle, using precise dissection techniques to separate the adhered structures.
Abscess fluid is visibly expelled from the fallopian tube during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the remaining portions of the fallopian tube from the vaginal cuff, completing the dissection and separation process.
The surgeon places the fallopian tube into an endocatch bag for removal, ensuring containment of the tissue during extraction.
The surgeon removes remnants of the fallopian tube from the vaginal cuff, ensures hemostasis, and closes the vaginal cuff using a figure-of-eight imbricating Vicryl suture.
The surgeon completes a modified McCall coloplasty to address a wide cul-de-sac, aiming to prevent vaginal cuff prolapse.
The surgeon places a piece of inter-seat adhesion barrier over the vaginal cuff to prevent adhesion formation, ensuring a smooth surface over the surgical site.
The surgeon moistens the surgical area with saline, indicating the completion of the hysterectomy procedure.
The patient recovered fully without any residual discharge and has had pain-free intercourse.
